clean_story:
Invisible trash. Dirty data streams.

Data pollution sources are the hidden factories churning out digital waste—just like smokestacks foul air, but with bits instead of soot.

Every "like," abandoned cart, or auto-played video leaves a trail: duplicate records, outdated info, or bot-generated noise. A single unchecked sensor in a smart city can spew 10,000 faulty readings daily, like a broken faucet flooding a database. Worse, AI trained on polluted data learns biases—imagine teaching a chef to cook using only expired ingredients.

Two major culprits:
1. Zombie data: Old files no one deletes (e.g., a hospital keeping 20-year-old patient scans clogging servers).
2. Shadow IT: Employees using unapproved apps (like sharing spreadsheets via personal Dropbox), creating silos of unmonitored junk.

Cleanup tools exist—deduplication scripts, "data decay" trackers—but most companies ignore them until errors cost millions. Like ignoring a landfill until it poisons the water.

Three related subjects are dark data costs, AI training garbage, and digital hoarding psychology.
